content.full_text:
R·G·KNOX
CORPORATION

Investment Management Services

1989 Mountain Boulevard, Suite 214
Oakland, California 94611
510/339-3800
FAX 510/339-2864

March 5, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Commissioners:

SUBJECT: Request For Ruling: Entertainment Expense

While reviewing Alameda County records involving
Supervisor Mary King, I found that I may have ensnared
myself by failing to report a gift. The issue has to do
with a bond rating trip that I took, along with several
other officials, to New York City in November 1988. While
recently reviewing records of this trip that were not
available at the time I retired from the Authority, I
discovered that I may have received benefits in the form of
entertainment that exceeded the reporting threshold of $50.

Section 87200-87210 of the Political Reform Act
requires that elected officials file an annual report that
states his or her interest in real property and his or her
income. Section 87207 defines the nature of reportable
gifts and income. A gift must be reported if it amounts to
$50 or more in value.

In November 1988, I traveled at the request of the
Alameda County Transportation Authority, of which I was its
Chairperson, to New York City to meet with the bond rating
agencies. A group of approximately ten of us accompanied Mr.
Grigsby, the Authority's financial advisor, to a Broadway
show, and then accompanied him later to a night club. There
was also a dinner involved, which I will discuss in a
moment. At the time of trip, I considered the gift
limitations imposed by the Political Reform Act, and I
thought that I had not exceeded them. I did not imbibe any
alcoholic beverages at the night club, therefore, I did not
think that I had r·ceived anything of value. After recently
reviewing Authority records, I believe that I probably
exceeded the $50 reporting limit, but there is an
extenuating circumstance.

Because of my recent review of the Authority's records,
I found the Financial Advisor, Grigsby, Brandford, Powell,
Inc., had billed the Authority in February 1989 for these
entertainment costs, which violates the Authority's by-laws.
I discovered in speaking with current members of the

Member National Association of Securities Dealers · NASD®

Letter to: Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Request For Ruling: Entertainment Expenses

Page 2 of 3

Authority staff that these expenses were not paid until July
1989, because they were in dispute for several months. Since
I had retired at the end of 1988 from the Authority, I was
completely unaware of this. Upon learning this, the current
Authority officials have made Mr. Grigsby reimburse them
for, at least, some of these expenses.

At the time of the trip, I believed that the theater
tickets were approximately $25. I do not remember that
Grigsby, Brandford bought dinner, but the receipts suggest
that they did. Moreover, the visit to a nightclub did not
seem reportable to me at the time, since I did not drink. I
only went along because I felt obligated as the Chair not to
snub the host in front of his other guests. I particularly
remember this because I jogged around Central Park the next
day, which I could not have done if I had anything to drink
the night before.

Having become considerably more familiar with the
Political Reform Act since November 1988, I realize that I
should have reported these events, especially after
reviewing the Authority records. The theater tickets,
according to the Authority records, were $50 each. Mr.
Grigsby claims to have spent over $200 at the nightclub we
attended, though the dates on the receipts he presented to
the Authority do not correspond to the dates that I was in
New York.¹ There was over $1,000 charged for limousines,
though, so far, he has not provided the Authority with a
detailed bill for the limousines. Finally, he claimed to
have bought the Authority dinner, though the receipt he
presented the Authority is illegible. My recollection was
that someone else hosted this dinner.

There are several issues that I am unable to answer:

1.    Since I believed that I received less than $50
from Grigsby, Brandford, Powell during this trip,
have I violated the Act?

2.    Since the Authority has not made a public
determination of what portion of the expenses they
will require Mr. Grigsby to pay, how soon am I
compelled to amend my Form 721 for 1988?

_______________________
¹The Authority is presently investigating the validity of
these receipts.

Letter to: Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Request For Ruling: Entertainment Expenses

Page 3 of 3

     3.    Since, in my opinion, I did not received anything
           of value from Mr. Grigsby beyond a free theater
           ticket, am I required to report any of the
           nightclub expenses, including the cost of getting
           to and from these locations?

     4.    What happens if the Authority decides that the
           expenses in question were properly charged to it?
           Would I still be required to report a gift², or
           would it be considered reimbursement for normal
           travel expenses under Section 82030(b)(2)?

     If you have any questions, please call me at (510)339-
3800.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Robert C. Knox

F:\G4\FPC\03092


²The Act gives the recipient of a gift thirty days to return
it.  Section 82028(b)(2).  Grigsby, Brandford billed the
Authority in February 89, for an expense that occurred in
November 1988, and he was not reimbursed until July 89.

sections.question: 1. Since I believed that I received less than $50 from Grigsby, Brandford, powell during this trip, have I violated the ASLII? 2. Since the Authority has not made a public determination of what portion of the expenses they will require Mr. Grigsby to pay, how can I be compelled to amend my Form 711 for 1988? 3. Since, in my opinion, I did not received anything of value from Mr. Grigsby beyond a free theater ticket, am I required to report any of the nightclub expenses, including the cost of getting to and from these locations? 4. What happens if the Authority decides that the expenses in question were properly charged to it? Would I still be required to report a gift, or would it be considered reimbursement for normal travel expenses under Section 82030(b)(2)?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks guidance on whether entertainment expenses from a 1988 bond rating trip to New York City constitute reportable gifts under the Political Reform Act, and how to properly amend disclosure filings given uncertainty about the actual costs and the Authority's determination of improper billing.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. Document is poorly scanned with OCR errors throughout. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections exist; the questions are embedded in numbered list format near end of document. The requester provides background facts and specific statutory references (Sections 87200-87210, 87207, 82030(b)(2), 82028(b)(2)) to support inquiry.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: 1. Since I believed that I received less than $50 from Grigsby, Brandford, powell during this trip, have I violated the ASLII? 2. Since the Authority has not made a public determination of what portion of the expenses they will require Mr. Grigsby to pay, how can I be compelled to amend my Form 711 for 1988? 3. Since, in my opinion, I did not received anything of value from Mr. Grigsby beyond a free theater ticket, am I required to report any of the nightclub expenses, including the cost of getting to and from these locations? 4. What happens if the Authority decides that the expenses in question were properly charged to it? Would I still be required to report a gift, or would it be considered reimbursement for normal travel expenses under Section 82030(b)(2)?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from R.O. Knox, former Chairperson of the Alameda County Transportation Authority, seeking FPPC ruling on whether entertainment expenses (theater, nightclub, dinner) from a November 1988 trip to New York City must be reported as gifts under the Political Reform Act's $50 reporting threshold, given discrepancies between his initial understanding and later-discovered Authority records.
